nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the island of Borneo, where both described species are each known from a single local population on different soil types — one on sandstone, one on basalt — roughly 600 km apart. Both grow as rheophytes, clinging to rocks and submerged wood along rainforest streams and rivers, and are naturally exposed to alternating submersed and emersed conditions as water levels rise and fall with the seasons.

decorPreferences: lava rock, seiryu stone, driftwood or other porous hardscape for the rhizome to attach to
gentle to moderate flow across the rhizome to mimic Bornean rheophyte streams
low to medium light; tolerates midground placement
stable, established tank with minimal parameter swings
high humidity if grown emersed in paludarium or terrarium culture

goodWith: Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp, which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light epiphytes such as Anubias, Bucephalandra, Aridarum, Java fern and mosses
avoidWith: large herbivorous or rhizome-disturbing fish such as goldfish, silver dollars and large plecos
vigorous diggers (large cichlids) that may dislodge attached rhizomes
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

summary: Fenestratarum is an exceptionally rare Bornean rheophytic aroid available only through specialty plant retailers. The rhizome must be attached to rock or driftwood and never buried in substrate. Low to medium light, stable water, and a well-established tank suit it best.
careNotes:
Fenestratarum is a small genus of rheophytic aroids in the family Araceae, closely related to Bucephalandra and Aridarum — all three share the same Bornean stream-and-river niche, and care in the aquarium follows the same principles. The genus is considered an epiphyte: the creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem from which the leaves emerge) must be attached to hardscape rather than planted into substrate. Lava rock, seiryu stone, slate, or driftwood all work well as anchor points; cyanoacrylate gel, fine sewing thread, or fishing line are commonly used to secure the plant until it grips on its own. DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME — covering it with substrate leads to rot and plant loss.

Fenestratarum produces slender, deep-green leaves that are slightly leathery in texture. Growth is slow. The plant tolerates low to medium lighting and does not demand CO2 injection, though supplemental CO2 tends to support steadier, healthier growth. Specific hardness targets are not well-documented for the genus; a temperature range of around 22–28 °C and pH of 6.0–7.5 — consistent with its Bornean rheophyte habitat and the well-established baselines for closely related Bucephalandra — is generally considered appropriate.

Like Bucephalandra and Aridarum, Fenestratarum is sensitive to abrupt swings in water parameters or lighting. Rapid changes can trigger 'melt,' where leaves die back suddenly. This does not necessarily mean the plant is lost: if the rhizome remains firm and shows healthy green tissue, recovery is commonly reported over the following weeks as the plant acclimates and puts out new growth. Fenestratarum is recommended for aquarists with prior experience keeping Bucephalandra or other epiphytic Schismatoglottideae rheophytes, rather than complete beginners.

The plant can also be grown emersed — with the rhizome attached to hardscape above the waterline in a paludarium or high-humidity terrarium — and is said to thrive under those conditions as well.
breedingNotes: Fenestratarum produces a small spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of the aroid family, and sexual reproduction has been described scientifically, but flowering and seed propagation are not practical in a home aquarium. The standard method is vegetative propagation: dividing the rhizome into sections, each with at least one or two healthy leaf nodes, and attaching each division to a fresh piece of hardscape.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Buce Plant's Fenestratarum culum product page: Borneo endemic, Araceae, rheophytic, slow growth, low-to-medium lighting, midground epiphyte that must be attached to hardscape and never buried, propagation by rhizome division, care level medium with sensitivity to abrupt parameter shifts, thrives in high-humidity paludarium/terrarium culture, slender deep-green leathery leaves. Specific temperature, pH, hardness and CO2 numbers not stated on this page.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales, Aroideae, tribe Schismatoglottideae), Borneo endemism, and that the genus contains only two described species (F. culum and F. mulayadii, both Boyce & Wong 2014) each restricted to a single local population on different soil types about 600 km apart. Wikipedia provides no aquarium husbandry information.
